item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a little article that does little with little. There's no central narrative, no threat, and no conflict besides some mildly depressing sentences here and there. And while normally these things would be essential for me to find an article captivating, there's a certain homely quality to this that make me want to keep reading. The edgy, depressing opening letter didn't make me cringe or feel seen; the darker, more emotional second letter didn't feel generic nor particularly inspiring; and the simple, straight-forward third letter didn't feel interesting in the slightest, but I never felt bored reading it.

I've flipped from upvoting this to downvoting it to upvoting to downvoting more times than I can imagine in my mind. However, I've come to the conclusion that this did accomplish what it set out to do and I like the destination that it arrived in.

This is a very tentative +1 from me. But don't be surprised if I reread this someday and change that back down to a -1.

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Review
item.content:encoded:
I'm just a bit torn on this piece. The premise here is good, very grounded. It has a deal of verisimilitude that I can appreciate in the same way someone write a personal eulogy on twitter for someone they idolize, but is no longer with us. The writing is also very strong in all three documents here. I particularly liked the small detail of the sentences in the third, emulating Hemingway prose in it's short, punchy splendor.

That is all to say that that's all there is to it. I was half expecting some sort of twist in the form of hate mail, or something so personal that the deceased author was responsible for something wicked. There's not really a rise in action here, which can make the overall pacing a flat line.

On one hand, I don't think this really needs much more than what is here; it serves well as three separate vignettes. On the other, I really would have liked a bit more personality from the fans here, as the user above me has pointed out. Something to really sink a connection with the reader would be ideal. For now, I'm no-voting.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to RockTeethMothEyes or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As the person above pointed out, the first part of this needs a touch more proofreading.

I agree with you that the letters are the strongest part of this piece. They create a poignant sense of the emotional connection between past and future that literature creates. However, I think that the emotional impact isn't an intense as it could be. I'm looking at the first and third letters in particular and feeling like they could reveal more about the personalities and histories of the "fans".

item.title: sending fan mail while your still alive
item.content:encoded:
To write three letters from three fans of literature, each written in there own style must have been quite an undertaking. There was a lot to love in this article so please forgive me for pointing out a few spelling and grammar mistakes. They are minor issues that don't really distract the reader from the quality of the work.
In the description "have been found to recently attempted to publish a work of literature" does not sound right. Were found to have recently attempted sound better.
In the letter to Hunter S. Thompson you wrote, “a group of power people are perverting the American dream” Did you mean powerful people?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Currently reading through this, but the font of the letters is rather difficult to read, such that I'm probably going to have to change it with Inspect Element.

item.title: Rewrite
item.content:encoded:
This is a rewrite of my previous entry for SCP-4462, having had some time to reflect on the criticism I've received in the time since I originally posted it back in August. The letters are basically the only remnant of the original article, but I feel they are the strongest part and this article puts them in a less distracting format where they can hopefully shine. The old article was just an unhappy marriage of two ideas, and I feel like I've explored the other idea fairly well in one of my other works.

I hope I didn't break any convention or rule by wiping the old article unannounced and posting the new version, but here it is, I hope everyone enjoys it.
